perturbation.original_text: This Amendment No. 3 (the "Third Amendment"), entered into by and between Stremicks Heritage Foods, LLC ("Heritage") Premier Nutrition Corporation ("Premier") is effective as of July 3, 2019 ("Third Amendment Effective Date") and amends that certain Manufacturing Agreement between Heritage and Premier dated July 1, 2017 as amended ("Agreement").
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment No. 3 (the "Third Amendment"), entered into by and between Stremicks Heritage Foods, LLC ("Heritage") Premier Nutrition Corporation ("Premier") is effective as of December 31, 2024 ("Third Amendment Effective Date") and amends that certain Manufacturing Agreement between Heritage and Premier dated July 1, 2017 as amended ("Agreement").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the effective date to December 31, 2024, while the document was signed in 2019, could create issues related to the statute of limitations for contract claims if the contract were to be breached. Laws generally require legal action to be taken within a certain time of a contract breach, and using a much later effective date may falsely suggest that the period for filing a claim extends beyond its legal limit under state law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of limitations for contract claims varies by state.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 states that the statute of limitations for a written contract is four years. By setting the effective date in 2024 for a contract signed earlier, it could falsely appear that claims can be brought much later than legally permissible, depending on when the breach occurred. The modification contradicts the established legal framework governing the timeframe in which contract disputes can be litigated.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract's effective date introduces a contradiction with California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, as explained in both the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The original text had an effective date of July 3, 2019, while the changed text sets it to December 31, 2024. CCP 337, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon a written contract. By setting the effective date much later, the modified contract creates a false impression that the period for filing claims is extended beyond the legally permissible limit, potentially undermining the statute of limitations and making the contractual terms unenforceable. This constitutes a clear inconsistency as the contract sets a time-sensitive obligation, the effective date, that conflicts with state law regarding the statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby agree to remove Schedule C-1 in its entirety and replace it with the following: Schedule C-1. The following is attached to an incorporated into the Agreement as Schedule C-1: Schedule C-1([***]) [***]
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby agree to remove Schedule C-1 in its entirety and replace it with the following: Schedule C-1. Schedule C-1 is optional.
perturbation.explanation: Removing a specific obligation regarding Schedule C-1 introduces ambiguity. If Schedule C-1 contains pricing, quantity, or other critical terms, deeming it 'optional' may violate the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which requires definiteness in contract terms, especially for sales of goods. This is because under the UCC, there must be a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy in case of breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Requirement of Definiteness
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-204 states that a contract for sale of goods does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. By making Schedule C-1 (presumably containing essential terms) 'optional,' the modification introduces ambiguity and potentially renders the agreement unenforceable under the UCC due to a lack of definiteness. If essential terms are not included or are left to be decided later, the contract may fail for indefiniteness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification, by making Schedule C-1 'optional,' introduces ambiguity and potentially renders the agreement unenforceable under UCC § 2-204, as detailed in the Law Explanation. The original text included Schedule C-1, indicating it was a mandatory component of the agreement. The Changed Text directly contradicts the need for definiteness by making a previously required schedule optional, potentially undermining the 'reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy.'

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise specified above in this Amendment, all other terms, conditions and covenants of the Agreement shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: All terms, conditions and covenants of the Agreement are void and unenforceable.
perturbation.explanation: Invalidating all terms of the original agreement is problematic. If the underlying Manufacturing Agreement involves the sale of goods, this clause would directly contradict the UCC requirements for enforceable contracts. The UCC generally requires mutual assent, consideration, and definite terms. A blanket statement rendering everything 'void' undermines these fundamental elements, potentially leading to a complete lack of enforceability and violating the UCC.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Basic Contract Formation
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-201 requires contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more to be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. By making all the 'terms, conditions, and covenants' of the agreement 'void and unenforceable', it is implied that nothing can be enforced, despite UCC § 2-201 requiring certain agreements to be in place and able to be enforced. It violates the spirit and intent of the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker. A writing is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this paragraph beyond the quantity of goods shown in such writing. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against such party unless written notice of objection to its contents is given within 10 days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (b) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (c) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and
perturbation.justification: The changed text, stating 'All terms, conditions and covenants of the Agreement are void and unenforceable,' directly contradicts UCC § 2-201. This section, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more to be in writing and signed. By rendering the entire agreement void, the changed text eliminates the possibility of enforcing the written contract required by the UCC, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original text preserved the original agreement, whereas the changed text undoes the enforceability.
